# Intragenic crop improvement: combining the benefits of traditional breeding and genetic engineering

### Affiliation

# Intragenic crop improvement: combining the benefits of traditional breeding and genetic engineering

### Author

### Affiliation

## Abstract

New crop varieties are developed by applying traditional breeding methods that rely on random genome modifications. These varieties combine multiple traits that support farm efficiency and acceptable yields but also contain genes associated with the production of toxins, allergens, and/or antinutritional compounds that were not considered during the selection process. Furthermore, existing cultivars frequently lack the functional genes required for specific sensory traits and the formation of health-promoting antioxidants. One new method efficiently addresses some of these issues by either silencing undesirable genes or enhancing the expression of genes that are linked to dormant beneficial traits. Rather than incorporating foreign DNA into the plant's genome, these methods transform crops with plant-derived transfer (P-) DNAs that consist of only native genetic elements. The genetic modification can be characterized molecularly so that any inadvertent transfer of undesirable DNA, as may be the case with traditional methods, is excluded. A recently developed intragenic potato plant is silenced for the polyphenol oxidase, dikinase R1, and phosphorylase-L genes in a tuber-specific manner. French fries derived from these tubers lack discolorations, display an enhanced potato flavor, and produce greatly reduced amounts of the suspected carcinogen acrylamide. It is argued that intragenic modification is unlikely to trigger phenotypic, biochemical, or physiological variation that is new to the species. Similarly, the targeted traits are similar to those that breeders select for and often have a history of domestication and reduced fitness. For these reasons, an updated regulatory system is proposed whereby intragenic crops are considered as low risk and should be cleared for commercial release in a timely and cost-effective manner. By using modern techniques to modify the same genetic material that is used by breeders, intragenic approaches may be perceived as an acceptable extension of traditional methods in crop improvement.

# How Genetic Engineering Differs from Traditional Plant Breeding

![Suzanne DeJohn](/globalassets/articles/gardening/content/8153-dejohn.jpg?$staticlink$)

I've been gardening and writing about gardening for more than 20 years, yet I find I'm always learning new things about the plants, insects and other critters that call my backyard home. That's the great thing about gardening — it's never boring! I've worked as a landscaper, on an organic farm, as a research technician in a plant pathology lab and ran a small cut-flower business, all of which inform my garden writing. Someone once asked me when I'll be finished with my gardens, to which I replied, "Never!" For me, gardening is a *process*, not a goal.

Last updated: March 11, 2024
| 6 min read

![Cereal label](/globalassets/articles/gardening/content/7926-cereal.jpg?$staticlink$)If you want to avoid GMOs, read labels and purchase organic foods.

Since the dawn of agriculture, humans have taken steps to improve plant traits, such as hardiness, taste, adaptability and beauty. Thousands of years ago, farmers simply saved seeds from their best plants for replanting. Over time, plant breeders developed increasingly sophisticated techniques to attain specific traits. The latest — some might say greatest — technique is genetic engineering (GE), and advocates say it's just the next step in humanity's long history of innovations for improving crop plants. Detractors insist that there is a fundamental and dangerous difference between conventionally bred and genetically engineered plants.

What sets genetic engineering apart from all other types of crop improvements is that it involves transferring genetic material from one organism into the genetic material of a completely unrelated organism — DNA from bacteria into corn, for example. This doesn't happen in other plant-breeding techniques.

Did you know that more than 70 percent of packaged foods sold in the U.S. contain ingredients derived from genetically modified organisms (GMOs)? If a product label lists anything that is corn-, canola- or soy-based — such as corn syrup, cornstarch or soy lecithin — you can be pretty sure it's from a GMO. However, according to 2010 poll, when consumers were asked, "As far as you know, are there any foods produced through biotechnology in the supermarket now?" 8% said "no" and 64% said "don't know." That's because manufacturers aren't required to label foods containing GMOs.

For both advocates and detractors to have a meaningful debate about genetic engineering, it's helpful to understand the evolution of plant-breeding techniques.

## Simple Selection

Traditionally, farmers saved seed from plants with favorable traits, such as high yield or better flavor, for replanting. In doing so year after year, the farmers created new strains of crops. Farmers in other regions did the same thing, resulting in different strains suited to their needs.

## Cross-Pollination

The next development in plant breeding was cross-pollination, which involves intentionally transferring the pollen of a flower from one plant to the stigma of a flower from another plant of the same or closely related species. Successful pollination results in viable seeds. When the seeds grow, with luck, one or more of the offspring plants will exhibit beneficial traits. It's a painstaking process because trait inheritance isn't a straightforward process ? crossing a disease-resistant corn variety with an extra-flavorful corn variety doesn't mean you'll get offspring with both traits.

Cross-pollination requires the parent plants to be compatible — that is, the same species or a closely related species. It's still an important breeding technique and it's the primary way amateur plant breeders create new varieties.

## Hybridization

Cross-pollination can produce haphazard results, so plant breeders looked for ways to ensure more consistent offspring from crossed plants, resulting in a technique called hybridization. The first step in creating a hybrid is to create two pure strains of plants by repeatedly inbreeding plants until a very stable strain is attained. Breeders then cross-pollinate these parent plants, yielding seeds that grow into uniform plants with predictable traits, called F1 hybrids. The drawback is that seeds from F1 hybrids won't produce offspring with the same traits as their parents. That's why gardeners who save seeds for replanting usually avoid saving seeds from hybrids, and farmers who grow hybrids must purchase fresh seed each year. Similar to conventional cross-pollination, the creation of F1 hybrids is limited to compatible plants, usually the same or very closely related species. The tomato variety Early Girl is an example of an F1 hybrid.

## Natural Mutations

Natural mutations can also create unique plants. When something causes a spontaneous disruption of the normal inheritance process — perhaps a "mistake" in DNA replication — the offspring (or even just a portion of the parent plant) can display different characteristics. If the mutation confers some benefit that makes the plant better able to survive, the trait may be passed down to subsequent generations. On rare occasions these mutations yield traits that are considered desirable to plant breeders. For example, the Red Bartlett pear is a mutation of the green-fruited pear; it tastes similar but has beautiful rosy red skin. Many variegated plants are the result of naturally occurring mutations that caught the eye of a breeder, who propagated plants from the mutated plant material.

## Induced Mutations

Observing how mutations can alter offspring, plant breeders began trying to induce mutations using irradiation and chemicals, hoping they'd eventually stumble upon mutations that resulted in beneficial changes. Star Ruby grapefruit and Ice Cube lettuce are examples of varieties created by induced mutations. Breeders also discovered that plants grown using tissue culture, during which plant tissue is cultivated using artificial nutrients in sterile conditions, are more prone to mutation than conventionally grown plants. Although the mutations are artificially induced, the DNA remains that of a single species.

## Creating New Varieties by Genetic Modification

In the early 1980s scientists created the first genetically modified organisms (GMOs) by inserting genetic material from one organism into the DNA of a completely unrelated organism, even a non-plant species. This breakthrough is the only method available to plant breeders to confer beneficial traits between unrelated species.

To create "Bt corn," for example, scientists incorporated genetic material from *Bacillus thuringiensis* (a bacteria that produces a substance toxic to caterpillars) into the DNA of corn plants. The result? Corn plants that resist corn earworm and corn borer, so the crops require fewer pesticide applications.

The next wave of genetically modified plants includes the "Roundup-Ready" crops. Scientists insert DNA from a soil bacterium to makes the crops tolerant of Roundup herbicide. Farmers can spray the herbicide to kill weeds without damaging the resistant crop plants.

## Benefits and Risks Associated with Genetic Modification

Developers of genetically modified crops defend their work, citing reduced pesticide use and improved yields. Genetic modification has also produced plants with higher nutrient levels than their conventionally bred counterparts.

Although genetic modification has been around for almost three decades and the U.S. Food and Drug Administration has deemed GMOs safe, many consumers still balk at the idea of mixing the genes of entirely different organisms. Wary consumers talk of "Frankenfoods" and express concern about hidden allergens and other undiscovered biological effects. Organic growers worry that the pests will develop resistance to Bt, an important organic insecticide. Environmentalists and farmers fear the creation of "superweeds" when pollen from herbicide-tolerant crops is inadvertently transferred to wild plants. And farmers who grow non-GM crops under organic certification or for export into countries that ban GM foods worry that genetic drift will contaminate their non-GM plantings. Crazy, paranoid ramblings? Maybe not.

## What's Next?

The first genetically modified food animal, AquAdvantage(R) salmon, is awaiting government approval. If it's approved, it would be the first genetically altered animal approved for human consumption in the U.S. Down the road, genetically modified plants could be used to produce pharmaceuticals. In the meantime, scientists continue to look to genetic engineering to improve crops ? for example, make them higher yielding, more nutritious, and have more resistance to drought and pests.

## What Consumers Can Do

The debate about the safety of GMOs continues, and many people have health, environmental and philosophical concerns about them. Responding to pressure from their constituents, many state legislators are introducing bills to require labeling of foods containing GMO ingredients. On a grassroots level, the Just Label It campaign is encouraging consumers to contact the FDA directly. If you'd like to add your name to the list, visit [Just Label It](http://justlabelit.org/).

Abstract
--------

Biotechnology has revolutionized agriculture through innovations such as genetically modified (GM) technology and CRISPR/Cas9 genome editing. In this review, a comparative analysis of these methods that addresses the concerns regarding the oversimplification of the notion that the CRISPR-based editing approach is equivalent to traditional GM approaches is offered. Since the 1990s, despite the potential benefits for crop improvement and food security, controversies have arisen around recombinant technology and the introduction of GM products due to perceived environmental and health concerns. In contrast, the recent emergence of the CRISPR/Cas system as a precise genome editing tool has garnered relatively less public resistance. In this review, the reasons for these contrasting findings are explored to shed light on the distinct characteristics inherent to each approach. Additionally, we conduct a bibliographic analysis to examine the shift in research priorities between the two technological eras, followed by a comprehensive comparison of the two approaches, to enable a better understanding of the potential synergies that can be achieved between agricultural biotechnology and conventional techniques for enhancing modern agriculture. A nuanced understanding of these technologies is crucial for informed decision-making, responsible implementation, and addressing the concerns surrounding agricultural biotechnology.

Keywords
--------

Agriculture

bibliometric analysis

CRISPR

genome editing

GMO

recombinant DNA

1. Introduction
---------------

Ensuring food security has become increasingly challenging due to the growing size of the global population and the ongoing impact of climate change. By 2030, the world population is projected to reach 8.5 billion, with a corresponding increase in the food demand, bringing the total to 11.6 billion tons ([Ahmad et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014124001675#bib4)). If current patterns persist, the number of individuals suffering from hunger is anticipated to exceed 670 million by 2030 ([FAO, 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014124001675#bib35)). Hence, an increase in crop productivity of 60-100% is needed to meet future food demands. However, achieving this goal presents significant challenges. The existing agricultural sector and food production systems face multiple threats, including climate change, land degradation, water scarcity, and the repercussions of the recent pandemic and political conflicts ([Lau et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014124001675#bib89); [Lau et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014124001675#bib90)).

During the Green Revolution of the twentieth century, human efforts in techniques such as seed saving, selective breeding, and variety breeding resulted in increased crop yields, quality, and production times ([Hamdan et al., 2022a](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014124001675#bib56)). These efforts notably increased the yield of major grain crops, such as wheat, rice, and maize ([Pingali, 2012](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014124001675#bib130)). This substantial progress in global food production has improved the socioeconomic status of numerous regions worldwide, contributing to the alleviation of hunger and malnutrition. However, newer challenges, which include limited arable land, an increasing world population, and an ever-changing environment, require innovative approaches to sustain agricultural output and quality at sufficient levels. In the late twentieth century, the advent of recombinant technology paved the way for the development of genetically modified (GM) crops, which hold the promise of improved nutritional value, yield, and/or stress resilience. Although these technologies are controversial, they hold significant potential to further boost global crop production and improve global food security ([Garland and Curry, 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014124001675#bib42)). Despite the controversies and concerns surrounding its development and cultivation, GM crops are currently grown on 190.4 million hectares by 17 million farmers in 29 countries, which represents a 112-fold increase since 1996 ([ISAAA, 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014124001675#bib67)). Nevertheless, GM crop cultivation and commercialization continue to face scrutiny, and these areas are subject to strict regulations in some countries ([Hamdan et al., 2022a](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014124001675#bib56)).

Concurrently, programmable site-specific nuclease (SSN)-based genome editing tools, such as transcription activator-like effector nucleases (TALENs) ([Li et al., 2012](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014124001675#bib94)), zinc-finger nucleases (ZFNs) ([Shukla et al., 2009](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014124001675#bib151)), and clustered regularly interspaced palindromic repeat (CRISPR)/CRISPR-associated (Cas) protein (CRISPR/Cas9) ([Shan et al., 2013](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014124001675#bib147)), have emerged as precise and versatile alternatives to conventional GM technology. Notably, the CRISPR/Cas9 system has gained immense attention for its ability to use single-stranded nucleases as molecular “scissors” to introduce double-stranded breaks (DSBs) in the plant genome, thus activating plant DNA repair mechanisms and introducing the desired changes ([Hamdan et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014124001675#bib58)). This enables a more efficient and accurate creation of new plant varieties with specific traits than traditional breeding methods. Proponents of CRISPR/Cas9 tools view them as highly adaptable and reliable tools for discovering novel crops with enhanced resilience, increased yield, and improved nutritional value ([Wang et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014124001675#bib181)). To date, CRISPR/Cas9 has been used to improve almost 120 crops ([Cardi et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014124001675#bib25)), including staple crops, such as rice ([Yuyu et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014124001675#bib191); [Alam et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014124001675#bib9)), wheat ([Zhang et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014124001675#bib200); [Gupta et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014124001675#bib52)), and potatoes ([Yu et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014124001675#bib189); [Alok et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014124001675#bib10)).

While the term ‘GM technology’ in this review is used to specifically refer to the conventional approach of introducing foreign genes into an organism’s genome through recombinant technology, ‘CRISPR technology’ utilizes programmable SSN-based genome editing tools. Despite the growing body of literature on GM technologies and the CRISPR/Cas system, comprehensive and objective comparisons of these technologies are still lacking. This study is aimed at filling this gap by thoroughly comparing both approaches. This is done by addressing the following questions: (1) What are the distinguishing characteristics between CRISPR-edited crops and GM crops, given that both share a genetic engineering foundation? (2) What factors contribute to the relatively subdued controversy surrounding the CRISPR/Cas technique in comparison to GMO techniques? The insights garnered herein hold implications for research, policy formulation, and public discourse concerning the integration and assessment of novel technologies.

2. Advancements in agriculture: A brief history
-----------------------------------------------

Agriculture, which is the foundation of human civilization, has undergone remarkable transformations throughout history. From the earliest cultivation of crops to modern-day practices, agriculture has undergone significant advancements that have shaped the ways that we address agricultural challenges and improve crop characteristics. The brief timeline presented in [Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014124001675#fig1) highlights the key agricultural advancements throughout history, including various techniques that have greatly influenced crop improvement and agricultural productivity. To date, various conventional and modern genetic modification techniques, which include crossbreeding, induced mutagenesis, polyploidy, protoplast fusion, transgenesis, and genome editing, have been applied in agriculture to produce healthier, tastier, and high-yielding crops ([Fig. 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014124001675#fig2)). These techniques represent the continuous human effort needed to manipulate plant characteristics to meet the demands of a growing population and evolving environmental conditions.

Fig. 2. Examples of plant breeding techniques in agriculture

**(A)** Crossbreeding involves selectively crossing different plant varieties to generate offspring with desired traits. **(B)** Induced mutagenesis employs mutagenic agents to induce controlled mutations, introducing new genetic variations. **(C)** Polyploidy manipulates plants to possess multiple sets of chromosomes, increasing genetic diversity. **(D)** Protoplast fusion merges the contents of plant cells from different species or varieties to create hybrid plants with desired traits. **(E)** GM technology (transgene transfer) involves introducing foreign genes into a target plant’s genome to confer specific characteristics. **(F)** The CRISPR technique (gene editing) enables precise modifications of targeted genes without introducing foreign genes.

Crossbreeding, which is an age-old practice, involves the intentional mating of different plant varieties to obtain offspring with desirable traits. This approach has been instrumental in enhancing crop characteristics, such as yield, stress resistance, and nutritional content. However, crossbreeding is a time-consuming process that often requires multiple generations to achieve the desired results ([Dou et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014124001675#bib31); [Van Vu et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014124001675#bib170)). Mutational breeding, which emerged in the mid-20th century, involves the induction of genetic mutations through radiation or chemical agents. This technique creates genetic variations that can lead to beneficial traits in crops. Mutational breeding has been successfully used to develop new crop varieties with improved characteristics, such as disease resistance and stress tolerance. However, the process of identifying and selecting desired mutations can be laborious, and unwanted mutations can occur ([Lau et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014124001675#bib89)).

Polyploidy doubles the number of chromosomes in an organism, resulting in altered gene expression and the potential enhancement of traits. This phenomenon can either occur naturally or be induced artificially through various techniques ([Madani et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014124001675#bib106)). In natural polyploidy, the genome of an organism undergoes duplication, leading to tetraploids (4n), hexaploids (6n), or higher multiples of the original chromosome number (te Beest et al., 2012; [Madlung, 2013](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014124001675#bib107)). For instance, tetraploid wheat (_Triticum turgidum_) is the result of artificial crossbreeding between a diploid wheat species and a diploid grass species, creating a hybrid that has four sets of chromosomes ([Rosyara et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014124001675#bib139)). Compared with its diploid ancestors, tetraploid wheat has larger grains and increased yield potential, making it a valuable crop for food production ([Zeibig et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014124001675#bib196)). On the other hand, artificially induced polyploidy involves the deliberate manipulation of an organism’s genome, which is often achieved through the application of chemical or cell fusion techniques ([Martinez-Fortun et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014124001675#bib112)). The cultivated strawberry (_Fragaria_ × _ananassa_) exemplifies polyploidy studies, as it originated from wild progenitors to become the most widely cultivated fruit crop worldwide. The recent publication of its high-quality chromosome-scale octoploid genome has stimulated new research directions, including genetic variant characterization and investigation of molecular control mechanisms ([Whitaker et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014124001675#bib183)).

Protoplast fusion involves merging the protoplasts of different plants and individual plant cells that have undergone cell wall removal to create hybrids that have combined characteristics. During the 1980s and 1990s, protoplast fusion became increasingly popular as a valuable tool for genetic transformation due to the development of efficient methods, such as polyethylene glycol (PEG)-based transfection ([Potrykus et al., 1985](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014124001675#bib132)) and electroporation ([Hauptmann et al., 1987](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014124001675#bib61); [Negrutiu et al., 1987](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014124001675#bib122)). Since that time, this technique has been widely used to generate novel germplasms and facilitate breeding programs, overcoming the challenges that are often encountered in conventional sexual crossing. Nevertheless, many challenges remain in the use of protoplasts for genetic improvement. One major limitation is the low efficiency of protoplast regeneration into whole plants ([Reyna-Llorens et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014124001675#bib137)). With the emergence of plant synthetic biology, protoplasts have the potential to accelerate genetic manipulation efforts, but the current techniques are still insufficient for realizing this potential.

The advent of GM technology in the 1990s revolutionized crop improvement. By 1994, the first commercially available GM crops were introduced, and their adoption quickly increased. As of 2014, more than 90% of the maize (_Zea mays_), soybean (_Glycine max_), and cotton (_Gossypium hirsutum_) plants cultivated in the United States were transgenic. GM techniques involve inserting or deleting specific genes, often from unrelated species, in an organism’s genome to confer desired traits. This approach enables precise modification and rapid trait introduction, leading to increased crop productivity, pest resistance, and enhanced nutritional profiles. However, GM technology faces controversy and concerns over potential environmental and health impacts and other issues surrounding its public acceptance and regulation ([Hamdan et al., 2022a](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014124001675#bib56)).

CRISPR technology has recently emerged as a powerful tool in agricultural research, as it enables precise gene editing within an organism’s genome. This technology offers more precise and efficient gene modifications than traditional GM techniques. It enables the development of crops with enhanced disease resistance, improved nutritional content, and increased tolerance to environmental stressors. However, challenges, including off-target effects, regulatory considerations, and public perceptions, remain, and these challenges often conflate genetically modified crops with CRISPR techniques ([Karlson et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014124001675#bib78)).

As technology advances, it is crucial to consider the potential disadvantages and societal implications of these agricultural advancements to ensure their safe and sustainable implementation. In the scope of this review, both GM technology and CRISPR technology share the common objective of implementing genome modification, yet they differ in the mechanisms through which DNA modifications are introduced. Understanding their key features and progress provides valuable insights into their research impact and agricultural applications.

3. GM technology: Key features and progress
-------------------------------------------

The hallmark of GM technology is its ability to transfer genes across species, thus enabling the introduction of desirable traits that may not be naturally present in the target organism. This is achieved through recombinant DNA techniques, through which the genes of interest can be isolated, modified, and inserted into the genome of the recipient organism. These genes can be sourced from the same or similar species (cisgenesis) or different species (transgenesis), which expands the genetic resources available for crop improvement ([Khan et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014124001675#bib82)) from those available using traditional techniques.

Cisgenesis and transgenesis are distinct approaches used in GM technology. Cisgenesis involves introducing genes from the same or closely related species, maintaining genetic integrity, and mirroring natural breeding processes ([Vasudevan et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014124001675#bib172)). Transgenesis, which is the landmark of GM technology, involves the introduction of foreign genes, which often leads to broader ecological and biosafety concerns. While cisgenesis is more closely aligned with conventional breeding, both methods offer unique advantages and challenges in crop improvement. The decision between cisgenesis and transgenesis depends on the specific goals of the breeding program, the characteristics of the target crop, and regulatory considerations. Cisgenesis has the potential to accelerate the breeding process through the introduction of desirable traits, such as disease resistance, drought tolerance, or improved nutritional content, more quickly and precisely than traditional breeding methods can ([Schneider et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014124001675#bib145)). In addition, this approach offers a means of overcoming some of the limitations of conventional breeding, such as the inability to introduce traits from wild relatives. Moreover, cisgenic crops may be perceived as being more acceptable to the public and regulators than transgenic crops are, as they do not involve the introduction of foreign DNA ([Dudziak et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014124001675#bib32)). Although more controversial, transgenesis has facilitated the development of traits like herbicide tolerance or insect resistance, which are impossible or exceedingly difficult to achieve through traditional methods. Both cisgenesis and transgenesis have been widely adopted in many parts of the world and have contributed to enhancing agricultural productivity and quality ([Marone et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014124001675#bib111)).

Unlike traditional breeding methods, which rely on the laborious crossing and selection of desired variants and often require significant time and space, GM technology allows precise gene editing at the molecular level, thus providing immediate access to the final product ([Van Vu et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014124001675#bib170)). The transformation process begins with the isolation and preparation of the transgene, which carries desired traits, such as disease resistance or improved nutritional content. The transgene is subsequently inserted into a vector, typically a plasmid, as a delivery vehicle for plant cell transformation. The introduction of the vector into plant cells is achieved through techniques such as _Agrobacterium_-mediated or particle bombardment-mediated transformation. Subsequently, the transgene is integrated into the plant genome via homologous recombination, which ensures the stable inheritance of the desired trait across generations ([Gelvin, 2003](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014124001675#bib44)). Achieving precise integration can be challenging, and thorough analysis and selection of transgenic plants are necessary to avoid unintended insertions ([Gelvin, 2003](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014124001675#bib44)).

GM crop varieties include more than 30 crops, such as corn, soybeans, canola, rice, and potatoes, which have been engineered to exhibit various beneficial traits, such as insect resistance and herbicide tolerance ([ISAAA, 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468014124001675#bib67)). For instance, Bt cotton plan

enriched_points.point_title: Combining genetic engineering with traditional methods
enriched_points.expanded_content: Integrating genetic engineering with traditional breeding enhances precision and efficiency, allowing for targeted improvements while preserving beneficial traits from both techniques.
Maintaining genetic diversity through hybridization and traditional methods reduces vulnerability to diseases and environmental stresses, ensuring resilient crop varieties for changing climates.
Modern techniques like CRISPR speed up the breeding process, enabling rapid development of drought-resistant crops by directly editing key genes associated with water stress tolerance.
Notable successes include intragenic potatoes that resist browning, which combine traditional breeding traits with genetic engineering, enhancing both flavor and health safety.

Future breeding strategies will likely focus on integrating advanced genomic technologies, fostering sustainable agriculture through enhanced resilience and productivity in crops facing climate challenges.
